points: A square can be doubled in area by constructing a new square on its diagonal. The diagonal square is made up of four congruent right triangles, while the original square contains two of these same triangles, proving the area doubles. This elegant geometric method avoids naive approaches like doubling the side length, which would quadruple the area instead.

A square can be halved in area by folding through the midpoints of its sides, creating a tilted inner square PQRS. A square with half the side length would have one-quarter the area, not half; the folding method correctly achieves exactly half. This construction is the reverse of the doubling method and demonstrates geometric symmetry.

By repeating the doubling construction, we can create a sequence of squares with progressively doubling areas: 1, 2, 4, 8, 16, ... square units. Each square in the sequence contains twice as many congruent triangular units as the previous square, illustrating both geometric progression and the composition of area through congruent shapes.

An isosceles right triangle with equal legs of length a has hypotenuse c = a√2. This relationship comes from the fact that a square of side a is composed of two congruent isosceles right triangles, and the square constructed on the diagonal of this unit square has double the area. If the diagonal (hypotenuse) has length c, then c² = 2a², so c = a√2.

The number √2 is irrational: it cannot be expressed as a terminating decimal, a repeating decimal, or a fraction m/n where m and n are integers. A terminating decimal's square would also terminate, making it impossible to equal 2.000... Proof by contradiction using prime factorization shows 2n² = m² is impossible for integers. The value of √2 lies between 1.414 and 1.415.

The decimal representation of √2 is non-terminating and non-repeating: √2 ≈ 1.41421356... We can find increasingly tight bounds by testing squares: 1.4² = 1.96, 1.5² = 2.25 → 1.4 < √2 < 1.5; 1.41² ≈ 1.988, 1.42² ≈ 2.016 → 1.41 < √2 < 1.42; 1.414² ≈ 1.999, 1.415² ≈ 2.002 → 1.414 < √2 < 1.415.

Baudhayana's remarkable method combines two squares with different side lengths a and b into a single larger square. Construct a right-angled triangle with perpendicular sides a and b. The square constructed on the hypotenuse c of this triangle has area exactly equal to the sum of the areas of the two original squares: c² = a² + b². This generalizes the doubling method to arbitrary square pairs.

The geometric construction places four congruent right triangles (with legs a and b) around a central quadrilateral by their hypotenuses. All four triangles are congruent, their hypotenuses all have length c, and the angles at each vertex of the central quadrilateral are right angles. The central region is therefore a square with side c and area c² = a² + b².

This construction provides a visual proof that the square on the hypotenuse has area equal to the sum of squares on the legs. Paper-cutting demonstrations show that pieces of squares with sides a and b can be rearranged to exactly cover the square with side c, making the relationship concrete and verifiable without algebra.

The Baudhayana-Pythagoras Theorem states: If a right-angled triangle has legs of length a and b and hypotenuse of length c, then a² + b² = c². This fundamental relationship is expressed geometrically as: the area of the square on the hypotenuse equals the sum of the areas of the squares on the other two sides. Baudhayana discovered and stated this in its most general form around 800 BCE in the ulba-Stra.

The theorem is called the 'Baudhayana-Pythagoras Theorem' because both mathematicians discovered it. Baudhayana (c. 800 BCE) stated it in the most general and modern algebraic form. Pythagoras (c. 500 BCE) lived about 300 years later and also admired and studied this theorem. The dual attribution acknowledges both contributions to this universal principle.

The most famous integer example is the 3-4-5 triple: 3² + 4² = 9 + 16 = 25 = 5². This right triangle appears in ancient mathematical texts worldwide. For isosceles right triangles where a = b, the formula becomes 2a² = c², giving c = a√2, connecting directly to our study of doubling squares and the irrational number √2.

A Pythagorean triple (or Baudhayana triple) is an ordered triple (a, b, c) of positive integers satisfying a² + b² = c². Examples include (3, 4, 5), (5, 12, 13), (8, 15, 17), (7, 24, 25), (12, 35, 37), and (15, 36, 39). These represent right triangles with integer side lengths. Baudhayana listed such triples in Verse 1.13 of the ulba-Stra, showing ancient mathematicians' systematic knowledge of these special triangles.

A primitive Pythagorean triple has no common factor greater than 1 among its three components. For example, (3, 4, 5) is primitive, while (6, 8, 10) is not. Scaling property: If (a, b, c) is a Pythagorean triple and k is any positive integer, then (ka, kb, kc) is also a Pythagorean triple. All non-primitive triples are scaled versions of primitive triples, and infinitely many triples exist due to this scaling property.

Primitive triples can be generated using odd square numbers. The formula (n-1)² + (2n-1) = n² produces triples when 2n-1 is a perfect square. For example, when n=5 (since 2×5-1=9 is a square): (4)² + (3)² = (5)², giving the triple (3, 4, 5). When n=13 (since 2×13-1=25 is a square): (12)² + (5)² = (13)², giving (5, 12, 13). This method generates many but not all primitive triples.

The abundance of Pythagorean triples for n=2 (where a² + b² = c² has infinitely many solutions) inspired French mathematician Pierre de Fermat (17th century) to investigate higher powers. Fermat's Last Theorem states: For any integer n > 2, the equation xⁿ + yⁿ = zⁿ has no solutions in positive integers. No perfect cube is a sum of two cubes, no fourth power is a sum of two fourth powers, and so on.

Fermat famously claimed to have a proof but wrote: 'I have found a truly marvellous proof of this statement, but the margin is too small to contain it.' His proof was never found, and the theorem remained unsolved for over 350 years, inspiring countless mathematicians and remaining one of the greatest open problems in mathematics. Many attempted proofs failed, yet the conjecture fascinated the mathematical community for centuries.

Andrew Wiles proved Fermat's Last Theorem in 1994, more than three centuries after Fermat's claim. Wiles's proof uses sophisticated modern mathematics (elliptic curves, modular forms) unavailable in Fermat's time and requires hundreds of pages of dense mathematical reasoning. This achievement represents one of mathematics' greatest triumphs, finally resolving a problem that motivated intense research across multiple mathematical fields.

The Classical Lotus Problem from Bhaskara II's Lilavati demonstrates the power of the theorem: A lotus flower with stem inside a lake has its tip 1 unit above water. When swayed by the breeze, the tip touches water 3 units away. The depth x is found using the right triangle formed: 3² + x² = (x+1)² (where x+1 is the total stem length). Solving: 9 = 2x + 1, so x = 4 units. This shows how geometric reasoning solves problems with seemingly insufficient data.

The theorem applies to finding diagonals of rectangles: diagonal d = √(l² + w²) for length l and width w. For rhombuses with diagonals p and q that bisect at right angles, the side length s = √((p/2)² + (q/2)²) = √(p² + q²)/2. For equilateral triangles with side a, the height h = √(a² - (a/2)²) = (a√3)/2. These applications demonstrate the theorem's universal relevance to geometry.

The theorem provides the foundation for distance measurement in all dimensions and fields. In coordinate geometry, the distance between points uses the Pythagorean principle. In physics, engineering, and surveying, any measurement involving perpendicular components fundamentally relies on this relationship. The theorem is not limited to triangles but underlies all geometric distance and spatial analysis.

Baudhayana's ulba-Stra (c. 800 BCE) represents some of the earliest recorded mathematical knowledge, containing geometric constructions and the theorem in its most general form. This predates Pythagoras by 300 years and shows that advanced geometric reasoning developed independently in ancient India. The preservation of these methods in vedic texts demonstrates mathematics' central role in ancient cultures.

The transition from geometric to algebraic understanding marked a crucial evolution in mathematical thought. Baudhayana expressed relationships through construction and area visualization; modern mathematics uses the algebraic formula a² + b² = c². Both perspectives remain valuable—geometry provides intuition and proof visualization, while algebra enables efficient calculation and generalization to higher dimensions.

The Baudhayana-Pythagoras Theorem is fundamentally important because it unifies geometry and number theory: geometric relationships produce numerical patterns (Pythagorean triples), and numerical properties reveal geometric structures. This interplay continues to drive mathematical research and demonstrates how different areas of mathematics interconnect at the deepest levels.
